title: Epochs, Batches & Iterations
concept: Epochs, batches, and iterations define the core vocabulary of the neural network training loop, tracking how data is partitioned and processed over time.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which loss function is best for a binary classification task?
exercises.explain: Correct! BCE is the standard loss for binary (yes/no) classification.

cards.title: The semester syllabus
cards.body: If you are studying a textbook, you don't just read the whole thing in one second. You divide it into chapters (batches), study them one by one (iterations), and when you finish the entire book, you start over again to review (epochs). This structured schedule is how a training loop is organized.

cards.title: The Training Vocabulary
cards.body: Three core terms define the loop: 1. **Batch Size** is the number of rows processed in one step. 2. **Iteration** (or step) is one forward/backward pass over a single batch. 3. **Epoch** is one complete pass over the *entire* dataset.

cards.title: The homework assignment
cards.body: Think of training like completing a workbook with 100 pages. If you do 10 pages at a time, your batch size is 10. Each 10-page block takes one study session (one iteration). After 10 study sessions, you've completed all 100 pages — that is one epoch. To master the material, you review the workbook 5 times (5 epochs).

cards.title: Calculating the loop
cards.body: A dataset has 1,000 rows. We set a batch size of 100. - **1 Iteration** processes 100 rows. - It takes **10 Iterations** to cover all 1,000 rows. - Once 10 iterations finish, **1 Epoch** is complete. If we train for 50 epochs, the model runs 500 total iterations.

cards.title: An epoch isn't a single step
cards.body: Beginners often get confused between iterations and epochs, thinking one epoch means the model adjusted its weights once. In reality, weights are adjusted at the end of *every single iteration* (every batch). A single epoch containing 100 batches will adjust the model's weights 100 times.

cards.title: Epoch vs. Iteration
cards.body: How data coverage is measured during training:

left.text:
One batch of data processed.
Weights updated once.
e.g. step 1 out of 10.

right.text:
Entire dataset processed once.
Contains many iterations.
e.g. completed review 1.

exercises.prompt: What does a single 'epoch' represent in neural network training?

options.text: One complete pass of the training algorithm over the entire dataset.
A single gradient update on one batch of data.
The time it takes to save the model file to disk.
The number of inputs in the first layer.

exercises.explain: Correct! One epoch means the model has seen every training example exactly once.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Model weights are only updated once per epoch, at the very end of the dataset.
exercises.explain: False! Weights are updated at the end of every iteration (every batch), which happens multiple times per epoch.

exercises.sentence: If a dataset has 500 rows and the batch size is 50, it will take ___ iterations to complete one epoch.
exercises.explain: It takes 10 iterations to process all 500 rows when processing 50 rows per batch (500 / 50 = 10).

exercises.prompt: A model trains for 5 epochs on a dataset with 4 batches. How many total weight update iterations will occur?

options.text: 5 iterations, because updates only happen once per epoch.
4 iterations, because the batch size is 4.

exercises.explain: Correct! Weights update every batch, so 4 updates per epoch * 5 epochs = 20 total iterations.

exercises.prompt: A dataset has 10,000 training examples. You set the batch size to 100 and configure training for 10 epochs. How many iterations will run, and how many times will the model's weights be updated?

options.text: 1,000 iterations total (100 per epoch * 10 epochs), resulting in 1,000 weight updates.
10 iterations total, resulting in 10 weight updates.
100 iterations total, resulting in 1,000 weight updates.
10,000 iterations total, resulting in 100 weight updates.

exercises.explain: Exactly! One epoch requires 10,000 / 100 = 100 iterations. For 10 epochs, that is 100 * 10 = 1,000 iterations total. Since weights update every iteration, we get 1,000 updates. Next, let's look at the tradeoffs of choosing different batch sizes!
